The New Zealand Attitudes and Values Study
New Zealand ranks among the world’s most tolerant societies (OECD 2011).

The NZAVS measures tolerance using validated “affective thermometers.”
However, Muslims are New Zealand’s least accepted minority group (Shaver et al. 2016) [Shaver et al. (2017); Hawi et al. (2019); Yogeeswaran et al. (2019); (Greaves et al. 2020; Sibley et al. 2020; Highland et al. 2019; Hawi et al. 2019).
The tragic 2019 Christchurch mosque attacks raised awareness of Muslim adversity, and were followed by gains in Muslim acceptance . This gave Muslims hope. Yet acceptance changed at different rates within New Zealanders, and among a sizeable minority, prejudice remained entrenched. The New Zealand Attitudes and Values Study (NZAVS) is a pioneering nationally representative panel study started in 2009 (2021).



Figure 1: Sampling from the electoral role, combines with repeated measures within the same people across New Zealand’s social diversity to affer confidence.

Figure 2: Within-person prejudice change pre/post attacks towards (a) Muslims, (b) #Immigrants, (c) Māori, (d) NZ Europeans. Redline indicates mosque attacks. Greenline indicates maximum warmth to Muslims, which was strongly boosted post-attacks, yet lags behind other groups
